items.q: Does light always travel at the same speed?
items.correct: Only in a vacuum; in any material medium (water, glass) light travels more slowly, which is what causes refraction.
items.misconception: Yes — c is a universal constant in every situation.
items.random_wrong: No — light always slows with distance from its source.
items.why: Students take 'c is constant' as 'c everywhere' and miss the qualifier 'in a vacuum'.

items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.

items.q: If one EM wave has a higher frequency than another, what is true about its wavelength?
items.correct: It is shorter — wavelength and frequency are inversely related (c = f x lambda).
items.misconception: It is also longer, because both increase together.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged — only the speed differs.
items.why: Students treat all wave properties as moving together (more of one = more of the other), missing the fixed-speed constraint that ties them inversely.

items.q: Gravitational potential of Earth at infinity:
items.why: Correct answer: 0 J/kg. “9.8 J/kg” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: 11.2 km/s. “3 km/s” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If GM/r = 6.3 × 10⁷ J/kg at Earth's surface, escape velocity (km/s):
items.why: The real figure is 11.2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
